T1566.name: Phishing
T1566.description: Adversaries may send phishing messages to gain access to victim systems. All forms of phishing are targeted, social engineering-based attempts to trick recipients into executing malicious code, providing credentials, or giving up sensitive information.
T1566.detection: Analyze incoming email traffic for anomalies. Look for domain spoofing, external email warnings, suspicious attachments, and unrecognized links. Monitor host-level process creation from mail client processes.
T1566.mitigation: User training, email authentication protocols (SPF, DKIM, DMARC), anti-virus scanner on mail servers, endpoint isolation, and restricting script execution.

T1566.001.name: Phishing: Spearphishing Attachment
T1566.001.description: Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with malicious attachments to execute code on victim machines. These attachments can contain macros, exploit documents, or executable installers.
T1566.001.detection: Monitor process execution spawned by email clients (e.g., outlook.exe launching cmd.exe or powershell.exe). Perform static/dynamic sandboxing on email attachments.
T1566.001.mitigation: Disable Office macros by default, implement application whitelisting, configure endpoint security to scan files upon download, and educate employees on document safety.

T1566.002.name: Phishing: Spearphishing Link
T1566.002.description: Adversaries may send spearphishing emails containing malicious links to lure victims to websites hosting exploits, drive-by downloads, or credential harvesting portals.
T1566.002.detection: Monitor network connections made to newly registered or suspicious domains immediately after a user opens an email. Monitor email logs for bulk identical outbound links.
T1566.002.mitigation: Implement web proxies, URL filtering, browser sandboxing, multi-factor authentication (MFA) to limit credential theft impacts, and URL rewriting/link scanning technologies.

T1059.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter
T1059.description: Adversaries may abuse command and script interpreters to execute commands, scripts, or binaries. These interfaces are common in administrative environments and are highly leveraged by threat actors.
T1059.detection: Enable script block logging (e.g., PowerShell Event ID 4104). Monitor command line arguments for suspicious flags like -NoProfile, -ExecutionPolicy Bypass, or hidden windows.
T1059.mitigation: Restrict script interpreter permissions, enforce constrained language mode, and implement application control policies to block unauthorized scripts.

T1059.001.name: Command and Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell
T1059.001.description: Adversaries may use PowerShell to execute commands and scripts. PowerShell is a powerful CLI and scripting language integrated into Windows, making it a primary post-exploitation vehicle.
T1059.001.detection: Enable PowerShell transcription logging (Event ID 4104/4103). Audit process creation logs (Event ID 4688) targeting powershell.exe commands.
T1059.001.mitigation: Deploy PowerShell v5 or higher, enforce code signing policies, restrict access to PowerShell using AppLocker/WDAC, and monitor for WMI and WinRM remote sessions.

T1027.name: Obfuscated Files or Information
T1027.description: Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents.
T1027.detection: Check for high entropy in files (indicates compression/encryption). Scan files for common decoding functions (e.g. base64, XOR routines, or custom packing signatures).
T1027.mitigation: Implement automated unpacking/sandboxing systems, enforce file execution restrictions on temp directories, and monitor runtime process memory for newly decrypted payloads.

T1105.name: Ingress Tool Transfer
T1105.description: Adversaries may transfer tools or other files from an external system into a compromised environment. Tools may be copied from an external server over standard protocols (HTTP/S, FTP, SMB).
T1105.detection: Monitor network logs for downloads of executable content (EXEs, DLLs, ELF files) from external IPs. Track command lines invoking `Invoke-WebRequest`, `curl`, `wget`, or `certutil` with remote URLs.
T1105.mitigation: Restrict outbound network access to authorized proxy servers. Block access to file hosting and code repository sites if not required for business operations.

T1071.001.name: Application Layer Protocol: Web Protocols
T1071.001.description: Adversaries may communicate using application layer protocols associated with web traffic (HTTP, HTTPS, HTTP/2, HTTP/3) to blend in with normal network activity and bypass firewall filters.
T1071.001.detection: Inspect web proxy logs for anomalous User-Agents, repetitive beaconing patterns (constant intervals), and connections to un-categorized or newly registered domains.
T1071.001.mitigation: Enforce SSL/TLS decryption and inspection at the network gateway. Restrict outbound web traffic to known-good reputation categories. Implement Host Header validation.

T1053.005.name: Scheduled Task/Job: Scheduled Task
T1053.005.description: Adversaries may abuse task scheduling utilities (like Windows Task Scheduler or cron) to facilitate initial or recurring execution of malicious code, ensuring persistence in a target network.
T1053.005.detection: Monitor Windows Event Logs (Microsoft-Windows-TaskScheduler/Operational, Event ID 106 for task creation). Track command lines utilizing `schtasks.exe` or `at` command line tools.
T1053.005.mitigation: Limit task creation permissions to administrators. Enable credential guards and restrict access to remote task scheduling APIs.

T1204.002.name: User Execution: Malicious File
T1204.002.description: Adversaries may rely on a user opening a malicious file (e.g., clicking on a spearphished attachment, running a downloaded setup program) to execute payload code.
T1204.002.detection: Track file-open events in user directories followed by rapid creation of sub-processes. Monitor web browser and mail client downloads.
T1204.002.mitigation: Disable autoplay/autorun on mountable drives, restrict user write access in system root directories, and apply robust local security policies to block double-click execution of dangerous scripts (e.g., VBS, JS, WSF).
